lines.text: We're {{ELAPSED}} in and the smoke's already thick enough to taste it. I'm sitting here at the PRGE desk — which is, let me be clear, a folding table and a whiskey bottle that cost more than my car payment — and I can smell burning plastic from here. That's either a structure fire or someone's making very poor life choices in a garage three blocks away.
The {{CLOCK}} clock says we've got eight hours and forty-five minutes left. The smoke doesn't care what time it is.
I pour a whiskey. You know what they say — in times of uncertainty, establish a rhythm. My rhythm is bourbon.

Earlier tonight, Caroline told us the cold front was coming in slow. Holden ran the numbers on how weather patterns affect activity rates — fascinating data, by the way, if you like statistics about how people die more efficiently in certain wind conditions — and what I learned is that smoke doesn't clear in a dead-wind scenario. The smoke stays. It pools. It finds every window frame and every crack in the door you didn't seal well enough.

I had a roommate in the '50s — the 2050s, not the decade my parents fucked in — who used to say that smoke was just evidence floating in the air. I asked him what he meant. He said, 'Evidence doesn't burn. It rises.'
That roommate is dead now. Not Purge-related. Just the regular kind of dead. Liver, I think. Or despair — sometimes you can't tell the difference.

We're getting reports from the East Side. Let me not read them too close. Let me just say: the numbers are moving in a direction I recognize. Holden'll have the detailed analytics when his shift comes back, but right now I'm watching the intake screen and the arrow is pointing up.

My phone just buzzed. A text from a number I don't recognize: 'The Beltline is not safe.' That's good information to have, I guess. Question is whether it came from someone warning me or someone bragging.
Caller: Derek from the Near East — go ahead, Derek.
Caller: Derek from the Near East — 'Quinn, I can see the fire from my apartment window. It's on State Street. The whole block is — I mean, it looks like it's spreading.' — Derek, are you barricaded?

Caller: Derek from the Near East — 'Yeah. Yeah, I've got the door locked and the peephole covered and I'm just — I'm trying not to watch but I can't stop watching.' — That's normal. That's the natural state. You're doing fine, Derek.
Caller: Derek from the Near East — 'How long until this is over?' — Nine hours. Less than nine hours. You can do nine hours.

Derek hangs up. I don't hear him hang up, but I know he does because everyone hangs up the same way — there's a beat where they're deciding whether to say thank you, and then they don't, and then it's quiet.

The fire on State Street is interesting because State Street is already a ruin. You can't really burn down a building that's been condemned since '63. So what we're watching is someone — some group of someones — taking active time during an emergency to make sure the ruins stay ruins. That's dedication. That's artisanal destruction.

I worked that block. In my previous life — the one where I was a functioning human with a career and a legislative aide and a mistress who was getting expensive at exactly the wrong political moment — I used to walk State Street and look at the buildings like they were teeth in a mouth nobody was brushing. Decaying infrastructure. Urban rot. Blah blah blah. Policy papers about revitalization.
You know what revitalized those buildings? A Purge-night torch and nine hours of no law enforcement. Funny how that works.

Another text. This time from my sister. Not during the Purge — she wouldn't call during the Purge, she's upstate in some compound with a generator and a bunker — but it's a pre-scheduled message that came through at 21:00 like clockwork. 'Quinn, I'm thinking of you. Please stay inside.' I reply: 'Not planning to leave.' She says: 'You always say that.' She's right. I always do.
Caller: Lisa from Willy Street — go ahead, Lisa.

Caller: Lisa from Willy Street — 'Quinn, you know how many people are in the neighborhood watch group this year? Forty-three. We are armed and we are positioned.' — Lisa, that's incredible. Forty-three is a real number.

Caller: Lisa from Willy Street — 'We've got the Yahara bridge locked down. We've got roof positions on the corner buildings. If anyone tries to push into Willy Street, they will meet active resistance.' — You sound prepared.
Caller: Lisa from Willy Street — 'We've been preparing since last March. We study the data. We know what works.' — And what does work, Lisa?
Caller: Lisa from Willy Street — '...Cameras that see in the dark. Discipline. The understanding that retreat is cowardice but advance is stupidity.' — That's a philosophy.
Caller: Lisa from Willy Street — 'It's survival, Quinn. That's the only philosophy that matters tonight.' — Stay safe, Lisa. Forty-three is a good number.

Lisa clicks off and I realize I'm actually holding my breath. That woman is the future. That woman and forty-two other people, armed and ready, defending a neighborhood block by block. Meanwhile, I'm in a radio station breathing smoke and drinking whiskey. The Purge makes philosophers of us all.

You know who didn't believe in self-defense? Senator Martha Calloway. My former boss, before the Vance Succession made her a non-person. She used to say, 'Guns are the symptom, not the disease.' Beautiful rhetoric. She said it from behind triple-locked doors with security she never saw because they were good at their job.

Calloway died in the '60s. Purge-related, as it happens. Not during her own Purge — she was protected that night, Level-10, bunker beneath the Capitol. But she lived long enough to see the Purge become law, become culture, become the one thing every person in this state talks about once a year. And then one year she just... stopped being protected. Nobody ever explained why.

The smoke is thicker now. I can see it in the studio — this weird gray-white haze rolling past the window like a living thing. Tim's checking the air filtration. He does this every year. Every year it's the same gesture, the same frown, the same 'We're fine, keep broadcasting.'
I pour another whiskey. That makes three. I'm keeping count because Caroline always asks and I always lie.
Caller: James from Capitol Square — go ahead, James.

Caller: James from Capitol Square — 'Quinn, the perimeter lights just went out. The floodlights around the Capitol grounds. I can see them from my building and they just — click — all at once.' — All of them?

Caller: James from Capitol Square — 'Every single one. The whole complex is dark now. I don't know if that's a system failure or if someone cut the power or if — I don't know what that means.' — It means the Purge is escalating, James. Stay inside. Stay away from your windows.
Caller: James from Capitol Square — 'The Capitol can protect itself, right? I mean, it's the Capitol.' — James, the Capitol is a building. Buildings don't have agency. Stay inside.

James hangs up and I'm thinking about the Capitol building. I've been inside it. I've walked those halls with armed security on either side. I've sat in committee meetings and voted for the Purge Act expansion in 2163 because it was good policy at the time, because it calmed the population, because I didn't believe the poor people would actually kill each other the way the science said they would.
They did. They absolutely did. The science was right. And I was in the Capitol when it happened, safe behind the same lights that just cut out.
The Purge Act passed by a vote of 47 to 3. I was vote number 28. I've had plenty of time to think about vote number 28.

Another text, this one from a listener: 'Thank you for staying on the air. I'm alone and scared and your voice helps.' No name. Just: 'A listener.' I don't know how to respond to that. I'm a drunk in a radio station. I'm not a help. I'm a byproduct of a system that decided entertainment during a massacre is a necessary social function.
But I type back: 'You're going to make it. Nine more hours.' Because that's what you do when someone says they're scared. You lie to them.
Caller: Reverend Tom from Marquette — go ahead, Rev.

Caller: Reverend Tom from Marquette — 'Quinn, we have forty-seven people sheltering in the church basement. Forty-seven. We've got water, we've got food, we've got prayer.' — That's good, Rev. How long can you hold?

Caller: Reverend Tom from Marquette — 'As long as God wills it. The locks are good. The walls are stone. We've been here since seven o'clock and no one's tried the doors.' — Keep watching those doors.

Caller: Reverend Tom from Marquette — 'I will. And Quinn? Some of these people are saying goodbye to each other like they won't see the morning. I need to know if that's reasonable fear or if that's despair talking.' — That's despair talking, Rev. Tell them the Purge has a schedule. People survive.
Caller: Reverend Tom from Marquette — 'I hope you're right.' — So do I, Rev. So do I.

The Reverend is my friend. Not a close friend — I'm not really close to anyone anymore — but someone I see once a year and who doesn't ask me too many questions. He gives sermons about forgiveness and second chances and the sacred nature of survival. He means it. He actually believes that God has a plan for the Purge.

I asked him once, years ago, 'What plan includes this?' And he said, 'The plan that requires us to choose survival every year instead of presuming we deserve it.' I didn't argue with him because he was right and also because I was drunk.

The smoke is definitely worse now. I can see it moving through the lobby on the building's interior camera — Tim's got a monitor that shows four different angles. The smoke is pooling near the stairwell. That's not good from an air-filtration standpoint but it's actually tactically interesting because if someone tries to use that stairwell, they'll be visible for a split second as they pass through the smoke layer.

We're in a building with security cameras and locked doors and a generator and Tim, who is the most competent person I've ever met in a physical sense. I'm going to live through tonight. The odds are good. Tell me the odds are good, Tim.
Tim, off-mic: 'Odds are good, Quinn. Finish your whiskey.'

Tim's right. The odds are good. I'm not Derek watching his neighborhood burn. I'm not Lisa on a roof with forty-two other people. I'm not the Reverend with forty-seven souls in a basement. I'm in a radio station with coffee and whiskey and walls and a door that locks.
Caller: Anita from South Side — go ahead, Anita.

Caller: Anita from South Side — 'Quinn, I just wanted to say that you saved my life once. Literally. You passed a bill in 2061 that funded mental health initiatives and I was in one of those programs and — I'm alive because of you.' — Anita, that's...

Caller: Anita from South Side — 'I'm not saying it to make you feel good. I'm saying it because I'm scared and I wanted someone to know that the person they're listening to — that you — that you've done good things. Even if you don't think so.' — Thank you, Anita.
Caller: Anita from South Side — 'You're welcome. Stay safe, Quinn.' — You too. Go barricade a door.

Anita hangs up and I realize I'm crying and I don't know when that started. I'm sitting at a folding table in a radio station and I'm crying because someone said I did a good thing in 2061 and I can't remember if that's true. I can remember the sex scandal in '67. I can remember losing my career. I can't remember if I actually helped anyone.
Tim hands me a tissue. He doesn't say anything. This is not his first time watching Quinn cry on air.

We're {{ELAPSED}} into the Purge and I've had three whiskeys and I've taken six callers and the smoke outside is thick enough to block out the streetlights. This is the part of the night where things get real. This is the hour where people stop asking 'Are we safe?' and start understanding that 'safe' is a concept that doesn't apply anymore.

The kill count from earlier tonight — Caroline and Holden had the numbers during their hour — is sitting in the low double digits. It'll climb. It always does. By the time we hit midnight, we'll be at forty, fifty, maybe sixty. By the time we hit 3 AM, it'll be triple digits and nobody will be surprised because we've had one-hundred-forty years to get used to that.

I remember when the Purge was new. I was young — this was the early '30s, I was in my thirties, which feels like a different person now — and people thought it would last one year. One experimental year to test if suspending law enforcement for twelve hours would somehow satisfy the collective human impulse toward violence and make the rest of the year more peaceful.

Spoiler: it didn't. But by then it was too late. The Vance administration had built the infrastructure. The government had marketed it. People had gotten a taste of it. And the people who benefited from it — the wealthy, the protected, the government-level elites — they had no incentive to stop it.

So it became policy. It became culture. Now it's so baked into the fabric of existence that children grow up knowing that one night a year is different. One night a year, the rules don't apply. One night a year, you stay home or you hunt or you protect or you pray, and whatever happens is just... the Purge.
Caller: Marcus from East Side — go ahead, Marcus.
Caller: Marcus from East Side — 'Quinn, I'm thirteen years old and I'm sitting in my basement and I'm listening to you and I need to know if this is normal. Is this normal?' — Define normal, Marcus.

Caller: Marcus from East Side — 'I mean, is it normal that I'm more scared of what happens after the Purge than what happens during it?' — That's very normal. That's actually the most sane fear you can have.

Caller: Marcus from East Side — 'Okay. Okay, I'm going to stay down here and listen to the radio and try not to think about tomorrow.' — That's a plan, Marcus. Tell your parents you called PRGE. Tell them I said you're doing great.

Marcus hangs up and I realize I'm listening to the next generation process the world we built for them. Marcus is thirteen. He was born in 2156. He has lived every conscious moment of his life in a world where the Purge exists. He doesn't remember a time before it. And he's sitting in his basement trying to convince himself that this is normal.
It's not normal. It's never been normal. But at a certain point, normal is just whatever you get used to.

Another text comes through: 'The fire has reached the corner of King and State. I can see it from my apartment. It looks like it's moving north.' I forward this to Tim. Tim nods and makes a note. Tim's job is to know where the fires are, where the danger is, where the safe routes are.
My job is to sit here and talk so people don't feel alone.
Caller: Patricia from Park Street — go ahead, Patricia.

Caller: Patricia from Park Street — 'Quinn, I just want to say that I've been listening to your show for six years and you've never talked about it but I know you lost something. I know you lost a lot. And I want you to know that you're still here and that matters.' — Patricia, are you safe where you are?

Caller: Patricia from Park Street — 'I'm in a house with three other people and we're locked down tight and we're going to survive this. But I needed to say that before anything happened.' — I hear you, Patricia. Thank you for that.
Caller: Patricia from Park Street — 'Okay. I'm going to go sit with my friends now. You take care of yourself, Quinn.' — I will. I promise.

Patricia hangs up and I pour whiskey number four and I don't mention it to anyone because Caroline already knows I'm lying and the only thing worse than Caroline knowing you're lying is Caroline having to listen to you explain why you're lying.

It's {{CLOCK}} and we are {{ELAPSED}} into the longest night of the year. I've got nine more hours to fill. I've got nine more hours to convince people that this is sustainable, that life continues after 7 AM, that the smoke will clear and the fires will burn out and the bodies will be counted and we'll all wake up tomorrow and pretend it didn't happen.
Except we don't pretend. We remember. Every single year, we remember.
I'm Quinn Foley on PRGE Radio and you're listening to Madison's Purge Night Broadcast. We've got nine more hours of this. Let's make it count.

tickerItems.text: Structure fire confirmed, King & State Street — moving north toward Capitol Square vicinity
East Side kill count climbing — reports from Atwood, Schenk's Corners showing elevated activity
Capitol perimeter lights out — cause unknown — no official explanation from Capitol security
Smoke visibility restricted across isthmus — wind patterns keeping haze concentrated in downtown corridor
